items.q: When an acid neutralises an alkali, what is the pH of the resulting solution?
items.correct: Approximately 7 if equivalent amounts of strong acid and strong alkali react — but it varies with strength.
items.misconception: Neutralisation always produces a solution with pH exactly 7 regardless of the acid and base used.
items.random_wrong: Neutralisation always produces a solution with pH 0.
items.why: Students learn the rule 'neutral = 7' so rigidly that they ignore weak-acid/weak-base cases where the salt is acidic or basic.

items.q: At dynamic equilibrium, what is happening to the reactions?
items.correct: Forward and reverse reactions continue at equal rates; concentrations remain constant.
items.misconception: Both reactions stop because the system has reached balance.
items.random_wrong: The forward reaction stops once the reverse reaction begins.
items.why: The word 'equilibrium' connotes stillness, so students think reactions stop — they miss that equilibrium is dynamic at the particle level.

items.q: What is reduction in terms of electrons?
items.correct: Gain of electrons (the oxidation number is reduced).
items.misconception: Reduction means losing oxygen and oxidation means gaining oxygen — full stop.
items.random_wrong: Reduction means a substance becomes physically smaller in size.
items.why: Students learn the oxygen definition first (from rusting/combustion) and never update to the more general electron-transfer definition.

items.q: How many atoms in total are in one formula unit of Ca(OH)₂?
items.why: The real figure is 5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
